Description: JWT bearer validation disabled in emulator accepts arbitrary tokens
Justification: TokenValidationParameters disables issuer, audience, lifetime, and signing key validation, effectively accepting any token presented via Authorization header or access_token query.
ImpactSummary: Allows unauthenticated users to obtain authorized access to SignalR emulator endpoints with arbitrary identities and roles.

Description: Hardcoded symmetric JWT secret in emulator source code
Justification: AccessKey constant is embedded in code and used to derive ValidKeys; leaked in console output, enabling token forging if emulator is exposed.
InjectionVector: Forge JWT with HS256 using leaked AccessKey
ImpactSummary: Allows forging of tokens and impersonation within emulator scope.

Description: Emulator JWT auth disables all validation (issuer, audience, lifetime, signing key) enabling token acceptance without verification
Justification: TokenValidationParameters sets ValidateIssuer, ValidateAudience, ValidateLifetime, and ValidateIssuerSigningKey to false, allowing arbitrary tokens (including via access_token query) to be treated as valid.
ImpactSummary: Authentication bypass within emulator: arbitrary clients can connect and assume any identity/claims because signature, issuer, audience, and lifetime validation are disabled.

Description: JWT bearer validation disabled in emulator accepts arbitrary tokens
Justification: TokenValidationParameters disables issuer, audience, lifetime, and signing key validation, effectively accepting any token presented via Authorization header or access_token query.
InjectionVector: Authorization header (Bearer) or query parameter: access_token
ImpactSummary: Authentication bypass within the emulator: attackers can self-assign identities/claims and access [Authorize]-protected emulator endpoints or hubs.

Description: Hardcoded symmetric JWT secret in emulator source code
Justification: AccessKey constant is embedded in code and used to derive ValidKeys; leaked in console output, enabling token forging if emulator is exposed.
InjectionVector: HTTP Authorization: Bearer <forged HS256 token> or access_token query parameter; AsrsSignature header for upstream spoofing.
ImpactSummary: Known symmetric key enables forging of authentication tokens and upstream signatures, allowing impersonation and potential full access when the emulator is exposed.

VulnerabilitySubType: Detailed error messages in HTTP response
Description: Exception message written directly to HTTP response in negotiate flow
Justification: ServiceRouteHelper catches exceptions and writes ex.Message/e.Message to the HTTP response, leaking internal details to unauthenticated clients.
InjectionVector: HTTP request to negotiate endpoint; response body contains exception message text.
ImpactSummary: Returns internal exception messages to clients, revealing service state and internal error text. This can aid reconnaissance and targeted exploitation.

Description: Unity editor inputs concatenated into ProcessStartInfo.Arguments and executed via Process.Start
Justification: Editor TextField values flow into MpcArgument.ToString() and are assigned to ProcessStartInfo.Arguments without validation or robust quoting, enabling argument injection into the mpc CLI.
InjectionVector: Unity Editor text fields for MpcArgument properties (e.g., Input, Output, ConditionalSymbol, ResolverName, Namespace, MultipleIfDirectiveOutputSymbols).
ImpactSummary: Allows manipulation of mpc invocation by injecting additional CLI flags/values through unquoted fields, potentially overwriting files or generating code in attacker-controlled locations within the developer machine.

Description: Certificate import helper caches CA certs without chain/issuer validation
Justification: CertificateHelper adds certificates from untrusted input into the CurrentUser CA store and returns X509Certificate2 without verifying trust, enabling acceptance of self-signed/rogue issuers for outbound auth.
InjectionVector: Configuration values: FirstPartyApplicationCertString, EventGridCertString, EgressV2ClientCertString; also CertificateHelper.GetCertificate(string path) if the file content is attacker-controlled.
ImpactSummary: Allows trust store pollution in CurrentUser CA store leading to acceptance of rogue/intercepting issuers for outbound TLS, potentially enabling MITM or impersonation of services relied upon by the application.

Description: Header-based client certificate acceptance without proof-of-possession
Justification: Code reads X-ASRS-Client-Cert-* headers and forwards them as clientCertificates without verifying they originate from a TLS client cert handshake, enabling spoofing/impersonation.
ImpactSummary: Allows client certificate spoofing: attacker-controlled headers are forwarded as ClientCertificates to upstream, enabling impersonation or unauthorized access if upstream trusts this certificate data.

Description: Unity editor inputs concatenated into ProcessStartInfo.Arguments and executed via Process.Start
Justification: Editor TextField values flow into MpcArgument.ToString() and are assigned to ProcessStartInfo.Arguments without validation or robust quoting, enabling argument injection into the mpc CLI.
ImpactSummary: No impact within this repository because the referenced Unity editor code is not present; no Process.Start with untrusted arguments was found in production code paths.

Description: Global missing authentication/authorization middleware enables unauthenticated access to all controllers
Justification: Startup.cs registers routing/endpoints without UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization and controllers lack [Authorize], allowing unauthenticated requests to reach sensitive actions.
InjectionVector: Unauthenticated HTTP requests to controller routes such as POST /notify/ingress-controller/shutdown, POST /notify/egress-controller/shutdown, GET /status, and GET/POST /v3/discovery:clusters.
ImpactSummary: Unauthenticated callers can invoke management actions (shutdown/reload), fetch status, and interact with Envoy discovery, potentially causing denial of service or unauthorized configuration disclosure.

Description: Unauthenticated sensitive management endpoint allows arbitrary SPU restarts
Justification: SpuManagementController.RestartAsync is exposed without [Authorize]/auth middleware, enabling anyone to trigger restarts for arbitrary ServiceIds.
InjectionVector: Unauthenticated HTTP POST to /api/restart with JSON body SpuResourceRequest { ServiceIds: [...] }
ImpactSummary: Unauthenticated callers can restart arbitrary managed services by specifying ServiceIds, causing availability impact and potential cascading failures.

VulnerabilitySubType: Detailed error messages in HTTP response
Description: Exception message written directly to HTTP response in negotiate flow
Justification: ServiceRouteHelper catches exceptions and writes ex.Message/e.Message to the HTTP response, leaking internal details to unauthenticated clients.
ImpactSummary: No observable impact in this repository because the referenced code file and negotiate handler are missing, and existing error handling uses sanitized messages (GetUserFriendlyMessage).

Description: HTTPS redirection and HSTS not enforced
Justification: Startup pipeline lacks UseHttpsRedirection and HSTS, exposing management APIs over plaintext and enabling downgrade/MITM risks.
InjectionVector: Plain HTTP request to exposed endpoints (e.g., GET /health) without automatic redirect to HTTPS.

Description: Unauthenticated shutdown/reload notify endpoints allow control-plane actions
Justification: IngressNotifyController and EgressNotifyController expose POST /notify/* actions without any auth; requests are broadcast via Hypervisor to service units, enabling shutdown/reload.
InjectionVector: HTTP POST to /notify/ingress-controller/shutdown or /notify/ingress-controller/reload; HTTP POST to /notify/egress-controller/shutdown.
ImpactSummary: Unauthenticated attacker can trigger shutdown or reload operations on service process units, causing availability loss and potential state disruption.

Description: Unauthenticated Envoy CDS discovery endpoint may expose internal cluster topology
Justification: EnvoyCdsController at /v3/discovery:clusters accepts GET/POST without auth; while intended for Envoy, unauthenticated access can enumerate runtime endpoints.
InjectionVector: HTTP GET/POST to /v3/discovery:clusters with body: {"type_url":"<envoy cluster type>","version_info":"0"}.
ImpactSummary: Unauthenticated clients can enumerate internal runtime endpoints (hosts and ports), exposing service topology and aiding lateral movement and targeted attacks.

Description: Emulator JWT auth disables all validation (issuer, audience, lifetime, signing key) enabling token acceptance without verification
Justification: TokenValidationParameters sets ValidateIssuer, ValidateAudience, ValidateLifetime, and ValidateIssuerSigningKey to false, allowing arbitrary tokens (including via access_token query) to be treated as valid.
ImpactSummary: If an emulator build disables all validation and is deployed, tokens could be accepted without verification, enabling authentication bypass. Not applicable to this repository as emulator code not found.

Description: Unauthenticated POST /api/restart in SpuManagementController allows arbitrary service restarts
Justification: Controller lacks any authentication/authorization middleware or [Authorize]; endpoint directly calls Hypervisor.ExecuteAsync(Restart) based on user-supplied body.
InjectionVector: HTTP POST /api/restart with JSON body SpuResourceRequest containing ServiceIds.
ImpactSummary: Unauthenticated clients can arbitrarily restart managed services by invoking the controller endpoint, causing availability loss and potential cascading failures.

Description: Lifetime validation ignores NotBefore (nbf) and accepts tokens not yet valid
Justification: Custom LifetimeValidator returns expires > DateTime.UtcNow and ignores notBefore, allowing future-dated tokens to be accepted before their validity window.
InjectionVector: Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header (token processed by JwtBearer middleware)
ImpactSummary: Allows use of tokens before their intended start time (nbf ignored), undermining issuer's time-based access control and potentially enabling early access or replay in certain scenarios.

Description: Reconnection token signing uses raw SigningKeySalt bytes; potential weak key if salt is short/predictable
Justification: SymmetricSecurityKey is derived directly from UTF-8 bytes of SigningKeySalt with KeyId as hash; if salt is low-entropy or predictable, tokens can be forged.
ImpactSummary: Attacker who learns or guesses SigningKeySalt can mint arbitrary reconnection tokens, enabling session hijack or unauthorized reconnections.

Description: Hardcoded symmetric JWT secret in emulator source code
Justification: AccessKey constant is embedded in code and used to derive ValidKeys; leaked in console output, enabling token forging if emulator is exposed.

Description: JWT bearer validation disabled in emulator accepts arbitrary tokens
Justification: TokenValidationParameters disables issuer, audience, lifetime, and signing key validation, effectively accepting any token presented via Authorization header or access_token query.
ImpactSummary: No impact within this repository: emulator file not found and JWT validation is configured with strict checks enabled.

Description: Issuer validation effectively disabled via custom IssuerValidator
Justification: TokenValidationParameters.ValidateIssuer=true but IssuerValidator returns the issuer without checking allowed tenants/issuers, so any token with a valid signature and audience is accepted regardless of issuer.
InjectionVector: HTTP Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header processed by JwtBearerOptions
ImpactSummary: Issuer validation bypass enables cross-tenant authentication where only audience and signature are enforced, potentially granting unauthorized access to management or data APIs.

Description: Custom TokenReader overrides JwtSecurityToken decoding; risk of accepting malformed tokens if future changes bypass signature validation
Justification: TokenValidationParameters.TokenReader is overridden to a custom ReadToken that returns a compatible token object; while signature is still validated later, custom decoding increases complexity and risk of parser inconsistencies.
ImpactSummary: No present impact: tokens still require signature and claim validation before use. Parser customization preserves original claims but does not enable acceptance of invalid tokens.

Description: Accepting Microsoft Entra public keys implies accepting any tenant without validating tenant ID
Justification: Signing keys are loaded from the common OpenID configuration and AzureIdentityFromMicrosoftEntraToken parses 'tid', but no tenant allowlist is enforced; any tenant with valid AAD-signed token and allowed audience passes.
ImpactSummary: Accepting Microsoft Entra public keys from 'common' without restricting issuer/tenant enables cross-tenant authentication. Attackers from unrelated tenants can gain authenticated context if they can obtain a token for the accepted audience, potentially reaching internal RBAC checks and escalating depending on configuration.
